topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut behind me, the sound echoing in the sterile waiting room. My palms were slick against the worn leather of my messenger bag. I scanned the room, cataloging exits, assessing the placement of every chair. This was it. Dr. Thorne. My tormentor from middle school, now a respected figure in cognitive behavioral therapy. A strange, unsettling convergence of past and present. I kept shifting my weight, the movement a nervous tick I couldn't seem to shake. Every glance toward the door felt like a calculated risk. I needed to appear composed, to project an indifference I absolutely did not feel churning beneath my skin. When he finally emerged, impeccably dressed and radiating a calm authority, his eyes swept the room, and for a split second, they locked onto mine. A flicker—was it recognition? Or something colder?

[story 2]

I kept my phone in my pocket, my knuckles white against the smooth glass. The air in the office was thick with the scent of old paper and expensive sandalwood. Every time the door opened, my breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake that I tried desperately to mask with a steadying exhale. I watched him across the expanse of the reception area, noting the precise way he adjusted his tie, the almost unnerving stillness of his posture. My jaw felt tight, a hard knot of tension that refused to loosen. I rehearsed opening statements in my head—polite, detached, utterly unremarkable. But the sheer weight of knowing this person, the one who once orchestrated my daily humiliations, was now someone entrusted with my emotional landscape made my throat constrict. I focused intently on a water stain on the ceiling, anything to avoid meeting that gaze again.

[story 3]

The hallway felt too narrow, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, high-pitched whine. I walked with deliberate, measured steps, my gaze flicking constantly toward the closed office door. My shoulders were hiked up near my ears, a physical manifestation of the internal alarm bell ringing incessantly. I could feel the energy radiating off the door, a palpable sense of controlled power that made the hairs on my arms prickle. I swallowed hard, tasting metallic fear. This encounter felt less like a consultation and more like an interrogation. When he finally stepped out, moving with an economy of motion that spoke of deep self-possession, I instinctively took a half-step back, my eyes darting from his face to the surrounding architecture, searching for an escape route that didn't involve direct confrontation.
